devices.name: Nikon D500

sdCard.type: XQD / CFexpress Type B plus SD UHS-II
sdCard.minSpeed: V30 (U3) for SD; CFexpress recommended for demanding bursts

devices.whySpecs: The D500 is a pro-oriented APS-C DSLR with 10fps continuous shooting and 4K/30p video. It provides two media slots: one for XQD (later enabled for CFexpress Type B via firmware) and one for SD that is compatible with UHS-II cards. Any U3 / V30 SD card meets Nikon's ??ments for 4K and ?? stills, but in practice photographers pair the camera with fast UHS-II V60/V90 SD cards or CFexpress Type B cards to keep the ???? clearing quickly during long RAW bursts and to shorten offload times.

faq.q: Do I really need CFexpress or XQD for the Nikon D500?
faq.a: No, but they significantly improve the shooting experience. A good V30 or V60 SD card in the UHS-II slot is enough for 4K?? and shorter bursts. However, if you shoot long sequences of RAW images (wildlife, sports), a fast XQD or CFexpress Type B card in the primary slot keeps the ???? from clogging and makes the camera feel much more responsive.

faq.q: What SD card speed is safe for 4K video on the D500?
faq.a: Use at least a U3 / V30 card. That guarantees 30 MB/s sustained write performance, which comfortably covers the D500's 4K bitrate. Slower U1 cards may appear to work at first but are more likely to choke during long takes or cause ????????.

faq.q: Can I mix a slow SD card with a fast XQD or CFexpress card?
faq.a: You can, but it may create a bottleneck in certain ?? modes. When you configure the camera to write the same data to both slots for backup, the overall speed is limited by the slower card. For maximum performance, pair your D500 with a fast card in each slot or use the second slot for JPEGs and the primary slot for RAW.

devices.notes: Flagship APS-C DSLR with one XQD/CFexpress Type B slot and one SD UHS-II slot. Later firmware added CFexpress Type B support alongside XQD.

devices.name: Sony A7R

sdCard.maxCapacity: No official limit (SDXC up to 2TB standard)

devices.whySpecs: ????? 36MP A7R records only Full HD (AVCHD/MP4) video and does not offer 4K or very high bit-rate codecs. As a result, its media demands are modest by modern standards: any reputable Class 10 / U1 SDHC or SDXC card is fast enough for both stills and movie ??. Choosing a higher-end UHS-I card with better ??????s mainly improves how quickly long RAW bursts clear from the ???? and how fast files copy to your computer, rather than unlocking new shooting modes.

faq.q: Can the Sony A7R use UHS-II SD cards?
faq.a: Yes, but only at UHS-I speeds. The A7R's card slot understands standard SD/SDHC/SDXC media and does not take advantage of the extra UHS-II contacts, so a UHS-II card behaves like a fast UHS-I card in this body. It will still work, but you will not see the headline UHS-II transfer rates.

faq.q: Is a V30 card necessary for the A7R?
faq.a: No, V30 cards are not ??d for this camera. The A7R's 1080p ?? bitrates are easily handled by basic Class 10 / U1 cards. V30 cards are still a fine choice if you want to reuse them with newer, more demanding cameras later, but they are not a technical ??ment here.

faq.q: What SD card capacity is reasonable for the A7R?
faq.a: Because 36MP RAW files are relatively large, 64GB or 128GB cards strike a good balance. A 32GB card can fill quickly during a full day of shooting, whereas 64GB+ gives more headroom while still keeping backup and offload times manageable.

devices.notes: First-generation ???? full-frame Alpha body. Uses a single SD card slot and records up to 1080p, so media ??ments are light compared to newer 4K/8K models.

devices.whySpecs: Like all Raspberry Pis, the Pi 4 runs its OS from the microSD card. An A1 or A2 rated card is crucial for good performance. The A-rating signifies faster ???????? speeds, which translates to a more responsive desktop experience and faster application launch times.

faq.q: Is a V30 card good for a Raspberry Pi 4?
faq.a: A V30 rating is not the most important spec. Look for an A1 or A2 rating instead. V30 rating is for video and doesn't affect OS performance. The ??????????????? Class (A1/A2) is what makes the Pi 4 feel fast and responsive.

faq.q: What is the minimum recommended capacity for Raspberry Pi 4?
faq.a: A 32GB card is a good minimum for Raspberry Pi OS with a desktop environment. This leaves enough space for installing several applications and for project files. For more complex projects or storing media, 64GB or larger is ??.

faq.q: Do I need a fast card for the Raspberry Pi 3?
faq.a: Yes, a Class 10 card is the minimum you should use. While the Pi 3 is not as fast as a Pi 4 or 5, using a slow, non-rated card will result in a very sluggish and frustrating user experience. An A1-rated card is the best value for performance.

devices.name: Lenovo Legion Go S
devices.whySpecs: Legion Go S uses a UHS-I microSD reader, so peak throughput tops out around 90–100 MB/s even if you insert a UHS-II card. A U3 / V30 card with an A2 application rating gives enough ??????? and random performance to run Windows or SteamOS games directly from the card without painfully long load times. Modern PC titles are very large, so 512GB to 1TB is the practical sweet spot; anything smaller fills very quickly once you start installing AAA games.

faq.q: Will a UHS-II microSD card make games load faster on Legion Go S?
faq.a: No, the reader in the Legion Go S is limited to UHS-I speeds. UHS-II cards fall back to UHS-I mode in this slot, so they behave like a fast UHS-I V30 card but offer no extra in-device bandwidth. If you already own a UHS-II card it will work, but buying UHS-II specifically for this ?????? is usually not cost-effective.

faq.q: Can I run big open-world games entirely from the SD card?
faq.a: Yes, but performance depends heavily on the card you choose. A good A2-rated V30 card can handle many AAA games respectably, but open-world titles with heavy streaming (large textures, big maps) will still load notably slower than from the internal SSD. Treat the microSD more as an overflow library than as your primary performance drive.

faq.q: What SD card capacity makes sense for Legion Go S?
faq.a: 512GB is a good minimum, and 1TB is ideal for most players. A handful of current PC games can exceed 100GB each; with patches and DLC, a 256GB card can feel cramped very quickly. If your budget allows, a 1TB card avoids constant uninstall/reinstall juggling.

devices.notes: SteamOS-based Windows ?????? variant with a UHS-I microSD slot. Designed primarily for game storage expansion rather than raw benchmark speeds.
